feedback.text: The development of the digital entertainment industry over the last 10-15 years has been rapid and chaotic. It is therefore perfectly understandable that laws that require a long period of consideration and a huge amount of caution have not been able to withstand. But it is no less important. On the contrary. I am very glad that, despite the difficulties arising from such a complex issue, there is still an attempt to bring order and law. Loot boxes, confusing premium currencies, hidden charges and unfair subscriptions Plagen have been in the digital entertainment media landscape for years and must be restricted. However, at the time of adoption of these issues, a new type of user-adverse behaviour has emerged. The deliberate dressing and expropriation of digital property. Films, games, series, images and music that have been legally acquired and where a purchase transaction, not loan financing, has taken place, are now denied and stolen to users and buyers. Since the physical media, such as CDs, cassettes and DVDs, had such a mere possession, and these so-called hard copies were covered by the general laws on theft and abduction, there was no legal provision as to the content of those copies. You bought a copy of the King of the Leuven and then it was allowed to play it as often, in private life, as you wanted. This is because one had that copy. If you do the same on a website, the same rights should apply. Always guaranteed access to the product. Up to the point where you are responsible for his own fault, such as breaking the copy or the device it played. Now again, entertainment companies are deliberately disposing of this access without giving any equivalent consideration. No downloadable copy assigned to the user’s account. No e-mail or letter with the purchased instance of the product, no refund. It is as if you buy a car from VW and only because VW then picks up the car, a towing car arrives at a passing and then removes the car. To clarify, I do not think that in this example, VW is responsible for ensuring that the wagon is still available after production has stopped. I just think that it is in the buyer’s right to continue to access the product and is able to use his own ANtried and effort to keep the product in a condition that makes it more palpable. This can be difficult for a car, but it is relatively simple for digital media. If a firm offering servers for a video game that is necessary can no longer finance those servers, it is of course that they will stop them. However, in such a case, private users should be allowed to carry out private actions, such as private servers, or local modifications to the copy of the purchased product after the cessation of support for these games. Such as repairing an engine or re-installing a headlamp on a car. At the moment, however, it is the state that companies in the digital entertainment sector are counteracting and preventing all attempts, even after the support has been discontinued. This is extremely user-friendly. If it would have happened to the time when the product is still profited, or where the servers and processes are actively tracked, then this is absolutely cumbersome. But as it is now, it is the destruction of property and another point where developers’ users are mistreated and where a legislative change is needed to protect consumers.
